coredata.dc:description: Operando monitoring of internal and local electrochemical processes within lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is crucial, necessitating a range of non-invasive, real-time imaging characterization techniques including nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) techniques. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the recent applications and advancements of non-invasive magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) techniques in LIBs. It initially introduces the principles and hardware of MRI, followed by a detailed summary and comparison of MRI techniques used for characterizing liquid/solid electrolytes, electrodes and commercial batteries. This encompasses the determination of electrolytes' transport properties, acquisition of ion distribution profile, and diagnosis of battery defects. By focusing on experimental parameters and optimization strategies, our goal is to explore MRI methods suitable to a variety of research subjects, aiming to enhance imaging quality across diverse scenarios and offer critical physical/chemical insights into the ongoing operation processes of LIBs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Operando monitoring of internal and local electrochemical processes within lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is crucial, necessitating a range of non-invasive, real-time imaging characterization techniques including nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) techniques. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the recent applications and advancements of non-invasive magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) techniques in LIBs. It initially introduces the principles and hardware of MRI, followed by a detailed summary and comparison of MRI techniques used for characterizing liquid/solid electrolytes, electrodes and commercial batteries. This encompasses the determination of electrolytes' transport properties, acquisition of ion distribution profile, and diagnosis of battery defects. By focusing on experimental parameters and optimization strategies, our goal is to explore MRI methods suitable to a variety of research subjects, aiming to enhance imaging quality across diverse scenarios and offer critical physical/chemical insights into the ongoing operation processes of LIBs.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), widely employed as energy storage devices in contemporary society, offer remarkable advantages including high energy density, cycling performance, and the absence of memory effects. They have emerged as the preferred battery technology in various fields, such as electronic devices, electric vehicles, and renewable energy storage [1]. Nevertheless, the evolving societal demands have raised higher performance requirements for LIBs, especially in the electric vehicle industry. To address current concerns regarding limited driving range, charging speed, and safety, it is imperative to advance battery energy density, enhance fast-charging capabilities, and improve safety features. Therefore, conducting in-depth investigations into electrode materials, ion/electron transport mechanisms, and the aging/failure mechanisms of LIBs is of significant importance [2].

The development of advanced in situ characterization techniques often results in substantial advancements in understanding the LIBs system [3]. As a quintessential “black box” system, gaining a deep understanding of its internal structural evolution and material transport processes during its operation presents a formidable challenge. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) has assumed a pivotal role in in situ characterization due to its distinct nuclear selectivity, local structure sensitivity, and non-destructive attributes, rendering it an indispensable tool for exploring chemical reaction mechanisms [4] and ion dynamics [5]. The real-time monitoring of electrochemical processes using in situ NMR devices helps to elucidate the battery failure mechanisms [6,7]. With the introduction of a magnetic field gradient, NMR techniques acquire additional spatial resolution capabilities, which are commonly referred to as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). In comparison to other imaging techniques, the primary advantage of MRI technique lies in its ability to generate multiple contrasts based on the unique properties of target species, such as chemical shift
δ
, relaxation times
T 1
/
T 2
, and diffusion coefficient
D
. This versatility significantly broadens its utility in monitoring chemical reaction processes and characterizing species distribution [8,9].

In this review, we first provide a brief introduction to the fundamental principles of MRI technique and offer a concise overview of research efforts over the past decade in utilizing MRI technique for characterizing LIBs, encompassing their solid/liquid electrolytes, electrode materials, and applications in commercial batteries (Fig. 1 ). Then, in-depth analysis is performed on the current limitations of the existing techniques, and we summarize the approaches from previous research and propose strategies for optimizing practical applications. Finally, we will offer insights into the potential of achieving higher-quality MRI methods for LIBs in the future. The objective of this review is to provide readers with a comprehensive understanding of the distinct advantages and challenges of MRI techniques for LIBs while serving as a source of inspiration for research in new areas and the development of innovative methodologies.

Most of nuclei have non-zero spins, and are characterized by their spin quantum number I . Such nuclei possess intrinsic magnetic moments, examples of which include 1H, 7Li, 13C, 19F, and so forth. When the nuclei are placed within a static magnetic field denoted as
B 0
, they undergo a process of spin polarization. Specifically, the nuclear spin rotates around the field at a defined frequency, and this motion is known as “Larmor precession” (Fig. 2 a) and can be described by the following equation:
(1)
ω 0
= γ
B 0

Here,
ω 0
represents the angular frequency of nuclear precession, and
γ
denotes the gyromagnetic ratio of a nucleus. The gyromagnetic ratio is an intrinsic property of the atomic nucleus, and even isotopes of the same element possess distinctly different gyromagnetic ratios (e.g., 6Li: 3.9366 × 107 rad T−1 s−1; 7Li: 10.396 × 107 rad T−1 s−1). When atomic nuclei are irradiated with radiofrequency (RF) near their angular frequency of
ω 0
, the magnetic moment of the nuclei undergo rotation and relaxation, and this process can be detected in a form of RF. This phenomenon is referred to as nuclear magnetic resonance. Additionally, the electrons surrounding the nucleus generate a secondary magnetic field, leading to a reduced effective magnetic field experienced by the nucleus. Nuclei in different chemical environments exhibit varying precession frequencies due to the “shielding effect” caused by their neighboring electrons. Therefore, NMR experiments can distinguish nuclei in different local chemical environments [10].

The initial experiments of NMR were continuous-wave (CW) experiments. However, due to the long experimental time and low sensitivity involved in these experiments [11], they were quickly replaced by pulse Fourier-transform NMR (PFT-NMR) [12]. The advantage of Fourier transformation (FT) lies in its ability to decompose complex time-domain signals into distinct frequency components. Hence, in PFT-NMR, a RF pulse with microsecond duration is often sufficient to excite the target nuclei with a range of local environments, significantly reducing the required experimental time. As a result, the free induction decay (FID) curve, generated by the perturbed magnetization vector within the NMR coil, can also be precisely distinguished into different frequency components through the Fourier transform analysis.

Relaxation is another essential phenomenon in NMR experiments. In a non-equilibrium state, due to the perturbation from the pulse, the magnetization vector deviates from the z-axis, resulting in a decrease in longitudinal component
M z
and an increase in transverse component
M
x y
. When the system is no longer perturbed, it will spontaneously return to the equilibrium state. At this point,
M z
undergoes a spontaneous recovery, while
M
x y
naturally disappears. The recovery process of
M z
is referred to as longitudinal relaxation, and the recovery process of
M
x y
is known as transverse relaxation. For the most common scenario, the magnetization vector
M 0
is perturbed by the RF pulse and flipped into the xy-plane. The processes of longitudinal and transverse relaxation can be described by the following two equations [13]：
(2)
M Z

M 0

1 −

T 1

(3)

− t
T 2
T 1
and
T 2
are referred to as longitudinal relaxation time and transverse relaxation time, respectively [14]. In MRI experiments,
T 1
is a major factor influencing the duration of experiments because the magnetization vector often needs be recovered to its initial state before applying the next pulse. Typically, each RF excitation requires an interval time (
T R
) that is five times longer than times
T 1
(
T R
> 5
T 1
)
. A time efficient imaging pulse sequence known as fast low-angle shot (FLASH) method combines a low-flip angle RF excitation with a short repetition time, thus improving the imaging efficiency [15].
T 2
determines the resolution of NMR spectra and imaging. For a species with a transverse relaxation time
T 2
, the half-width of its NMR spectral peaks is given by
1
π
T 2
. Therefore, in the case of solids with shorter
T 2
, the NMR spectral peaks tend to be significantly broader, leading to a lower spectral resolution. This limitation, in turn, restricts the achievable resolution in solid-state MRI imaging [16].

While NMR spectroscopy relies on the variations in local chemical environments to discern nuclei, MRI employs additional magnetic gradient coils to endow nuclei with spatial information. With a set of NMR gradient coils, linear gradient magnetic fields are generated along all three spatial dimensions, resulting in different magnetic field strengths at different positions. Deriving from the Larmor precession equation, the resonance frequencies of atomic nuclei are in direct proportion to the surrounding magnetic fields. Therefore, when a magnetic field gradient is present, the difference in resonance frequencies for the same type of nucleus arises not only from different chemical environments but also from variations in magnetic field strength at different positions. In fact, for traditional MRI experiments, the frequency differences caused by chemical shifts are often neglected or even considered one of the sources of image artifacts [17]. Consequently, these frequency differences are primarily spatially encoded thereby facilitating imaging.

A classical multi-slice spin-echo MRI experiment’s pulse sequence consists of a spin-echo pulse and three gradient pulses applied in different directions (Fig. 2b). The magnetic field gradients along the three axes are known as slice-selective gradient
G s
, frequency-encoding gradient
G f
, and phase-encoding gradient
G p
. Through the combined action of these three gradients, the nuclear density within each voxel can be distinguishable. To be more specific, the signal
S
(
x , y , z
)
within each voxel can be described as below (neglecting relaxation effects):
(4)
S
(
x , y , z
)
= k
ρ
( x , y , z )
exp
(
i
ω

φ
( x , y , z )
)
Here,
ρ
( x , y , z )
represents the nuclear density within each voxel,
k
is a proportionality constant relating nuclear density to signal intensity,
ω
( x , y , z )
denotes the statistically averaged precession frequency of atomic nuclei within the voxel, and
φ
( x , y , z )
represents the phase of nuclear moment precession. While phase was neglected in the previous discussion, it plays a crucial role in MRI. Differentiating based on the combination of precession frequency and phase allows for the distinction of atomic nuclei within each voxel in three-dimensional space.

Slice selection refers to the process of exciting the atomic nuclei on a specific cross-sectional plane of the object under examination, which has a certain thickness
δ
. The key to achieving this lies in the combined action of selective RF pulses and slice-selective gradient
G s
. When the slice-selective gradient is applied, assuming its direction aligns with the z-axis, the precession frequencies of all atomic nuclei within the object distribute linearly along the z-axis. Because the excitation range of a pulse is finite, it allows excitation of only those atomic nuclei within a certain frequency range (Fig. 2c). The excitation center on region
z
and the slice thickness
Δ z
can be controlled via the central frequency
ω z
of the pulse and the effective excitation range
Δ ω
, respectively,

To improve spatial resolution, the slice thickness needs to be minimized. Two methods to improve slice selection resolution include: 1) reducing the effective excitation range
Δ ω
of the pulse, and 2) increasing the magnetic field gradient
G s
.

The most direct approach to reduce
Δ ω
is by using a RF pulse with lower power and longer duration, but excessively long pulse durations will compromise imaging speed. Alternatively, optimizing the pulse shape is another commonly used strategy. The shape of the RF pulse is critically important. A commonly used rectangular RF pulse corresponds to a sinc function excitation in the frequency domain. This results in non-uniform signal excitation within the slice and unwanted excitation outside the slice. Conversely, a sinc function-shaped RF pulse in the time domain, after being transformed via Fourier transform, becomes a rectangular-shaped excitation in the frequency domain. This is more ideal for slice selection.

The enhancement of the magnetic field gradient
G s
is limited by hardware. A larger magnetic field gradient implies a higher driving current and more heating, posing significant challenges for cooling systems. Moreover, a longer time is required for the opening and closing of currents; otherwise, eddy currents may be generated, causing image artifacts.

It is worth noting that as the slice thickness decreases, the population of excitable nuclei also decreases, leading to a significant reduction in signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and more sampling time for achieving desired resolution.

When the atomic nuclei in a particular slice are excited from one dimension, it is necessary to encode the remaining two dimensions within the slice to differentiate the signals from each voxel. Frequency encoding is a relatively easy-to-understand encoding method, and its data processing process is similar to NMR spectroscopy. Unlike slice selection, in which magnetic field gradients are applied during RF excitation, frequency encoding involves applying a magnetic field gradient during the acquisition of FID. This magnetic field gradient is also known as the readout gradient. Under the influence of the readout gradient, the precession frequency of atomic nuclei changes linearly along the gradient direction, meaning that position and frequency are directly correlated. Consequently, the collected FID signal is a superposition of signals from different positions. By Fourier transformation, one can obtain the signal distribution from various positions. Therefore, frequency encoding experiments can directly image selected slice along one dimension, offering the advantage of speed.

Similar to slice selection, the spatial resolution
Δ x
of frequency encoding depends on the resolution of the frequency spectrum
Δ ω
x
and the strength of the frequency encoding gradient
G f
. The resolution of frequency encoding is divided into digital resolution and real resolution. According to the Nyquist theorem, the digital resolution,
Δ ω
x
, of the spectrum is inversely proportional to the sampling duration AQ:
Δ ω
x
=
1
A Q
, where AQ is the product of each FID sample interval (
T E
), and the total sampled data points (
T D
),
A Q = T E × T D
. At first glance, it may seem that with longer sampling times, the digital resolution increases, implying higher spatial resolution in frequency encoding. However, it is important to note that the FID signal is decaying continuously. When the sampling time is excessively long, the latter part of the signal is essentially noise. Adding noise degrades the overall SNR, reducing image quality, nor does it contribute to the resolution. The real resolution of frequency encoding is determined by the decay rate
T 2 ∗
of the FID. From the perspective of NMR spectra, the half-width of the spectrum is
1
π
T 2 ∗
, and a short
T 2 ∗
causes the broadening of nuclear peak at the same position. Peak broadening makes it difficult to distinguish signals from closely spaced atomic nuclei, leading to reduced resolution.

High frequency-encoding gradient strength
G f
exacerbate field inhomogeneity, resulting in rapid dephasing of precession and fast signal decay, which in turn increases
Δ ω
x
. Therefore, in frequency encoding, using ultra-high magnetic field gradients does not necessarily lead to higher resolution. The complexity of factors that affect resolution also implies that, for a given object, the upper limit of the achievable resolution in frequency encoding is mainly determined by samples’ intrinsic properties [18].

When frequency encoding is applied to one dimension after slice selection, the remaining dimension cannot be encoded in the same way, as it would be impossible to determine which dimension’s frequency distribution is obtained through Fourier transformation. Therefore, an additional encoding method is introduced, known as phase encoding. Assuming the y -axis is the phase-encoded direction, when the phase-encoding gradient
G p
is turned on, the precession frequencies of magnetization vectors at different positions change. After a certain period, due to the difference in precession frequencies, the magnetic vectors at different positions have different phases. When the magnetic field gradient is turned off, the precession frequencies return to the initial state, but the additional phase is retained and recorded in the FID curve during signal acquisition. However, the phase information reflected in the FID curve cannot be directly correlated with position. Therefore, a series of signal acquisitions with varying phase encoding gradient magnitudes is needed to obtain images with good spatial resolution. Excessive sampling time limits the resolution of phase encoding. For phase encoding, its resolution
Δ y
and field of view (FOV) can be expressed as follows:

. As a result, to achieve sufficient resolution for a fixed FOV, a large number of phase encoding steps may be required, potentially resulting in significantly extended experimental times.

It is worth noting that phase encoding does not affect the precession frequency of the magnetization vector. When pure phase encoding is used for imaging, the frequency variations induced by chemical environments are retained. This enables the acquisition of NMR spectra with spatial resolution, known as chemical shift imaging, which finds wide applications in medical and chemical research [19].

The two-dimensional (2D) Fourier transform converts the FID signal after frequency encoding and phase encoding into a 2D MRI image. After undergoing frequency encoding in the x-direction and phase encoding in the y-direction, the signal can be represented as

During the data acquisition process, the raw data
S
(
k x
,
k y
)
is sequentially filled into k-space. Typically, the rows of k-space correspond to the
k x
direction, and the columns correspond to the
k y
direction. For each phase encoding step, the signal undergoes phase evolution, which is reflected in the acquired FID signal after frequency encoding. The acquired FID signal is directly filled into one row of k-space. For example, when the phase encoding is
+ G
p m a x
, it fills the first row of k-space; when the phase encoding gradient is zero, it fills the middle row of k-space; when the phase encoding gradient is
− G
p m a x
, it fills the last row of k-space. When k-space is filled, performing a 2D inverse Fourier transform on it yields the MRI image (Fig. 2d).

The characteristics of k-space offer the possibility to optimize the signal acquisition process and to improve the efficiency of MRI imaging. Firstly, each point in k-space contributes to the entire MRI image, but with varying degrees of influence. The central region of k-space represents the low-frequency components, determining the main part of the image, while the outer region contains high-frequency components responsible for image details. Additionally, due to the duality of k-space, it is not necessary to fully sample the entire k-space for image reconstruction, which reduces the time required for data acquisition. The introduction of deep learning techniques has also made it possible to achieve high-quality image reconstruction based on undersampled sparse k-space data [20,21].

A typical MRI instrument consists of a magnet, gradient coils, RF coils, and a spectrometer control system [13]. All being important, the MRI image quality is mostly affected by the performance of the magnet and gradient coils.

The magnet provides the essential static magnetic field
B 0
required for MRI experiments. As the SNR of experimental data is directly proportional to the strength of the static magnetic field
B 0
, the pursuit of a higher field has been a long-term goal in the field of MRI [22]. Currently, the majority of MRI instruments use superconducting coils to provide Tesla-level magnetic fields. In the case of NMR spectrometers used for chemistry research, the strength of
B 0
can reach up to 28.2 T (corresponding to 1.2 GHz for 1H nuclei). Medical MRI scanners typically use magnetic field strengths of 1.5 T or 3 T to ensure the safety of patients. In addition, maintaining the uniformity of magnetic fields in large regions poses significant challenges [23].

Gradient coils are responsible for generating the necessary gradient magnetic fields for MRI experiments. The simplest form of gradient coils consists of two parallel, equally sized circular coils known as Maxwell coil structure. When opposite currents flow through these coils, a linear gradient magnetic field is generated along the axis between the coils. To meet the requirements of high-resolution and fast MRI experiments, the strength of the gradient fields and the switching speed of gradient are two important specifications for MRI instruments [24]. Since the gradient magnetic fields are driven by electric currents, higher gradient strengths require larger currents, which may generate more heat and pose challenges in terms of instrument cooling and safety issues. Additionally, the rapid increase and decrease of high currents can lead to eddy currents, potentially resulting in artifacts. In the following, we provide a list of the instrumental parameters of commonly used MRI devices (Table 1 ). For medical MRI instruments, magnetic field gradients on the order of tens of milli Tesla per meter (mT/m) are sufficient to provide MRI-images with high-resolution (millimeters to sub-millimeters, for 1H nuclei). In contrast, for MRI devices used in LIBs research, where the detection region is smaller, the magnetic field gradients and gradient switching speeds can be much higher than those used in medical MRI instruments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of MRI for lithium-ion batteries

ce:section.ce:para: The electrolyte plays a crucial role in transporting lithium ions (Li+). As the demand for rapid charging performance in LIBs continues to increase, there is a need for further optimization of the ionic transport properties of the electrolyte. Poor ionic transport in electrolyte restricts the fast-charging performance of LIBs, being manifested as ohmic polarization and concentration polarization during high-current operation. Therefore, an accurate assessment of the ionic transport capability of the electrolyte is essential.

MRI technique enables the imaging of Li+ distribution within an in situ electrochemical cell under applied current, which detects ion concentration gradients under polarization conditions, allowing for the determination of diffusion coefficients and ion transference numbers when combined with mass transport models [31–37]. Klett et al. were pioneers in employing one-dimensional spin-echo frequency-encoded 7Li MRI to visualize the distribution of Li+ within the electrolyte under polarization conditions [31]. By collecting concentration gradients (Fig. 3 a–c) at different current density and fitting them to the corresponding mass transport model, they determined the lithium ion transference number successfully for a specific electrolyte (1 M LiPF6 in EC/DEC/PMMA). However, it is well-known that ion concentration significantly influences ion transport capabilities [29]. Thus, when concentration gradients arise, ion transport parameters inherently exhibit spatial non-uniformity. Krachkovskiy et al. combined 1D-7Li MRI and PFG-NMR and successfully measured Li+ diffusion coefficients with spatial resolution [33]. They measured the transport parameters of Li+ near the anode and cathode regions, obtaining results consistent with those measured in static electrolytes of varying concentrations: In a polarized condition, the region with a higher Li+ concentration (proximate to the anode) corresponds to a lower Li+ diffusion coefficient.

In the frequency-encoded image, signal distortions occur near the current collector (approximately 2 mm from the current collector) due to variations in magnetic susceptibility. Consequently, in situ cells often require a minimal length of 1–2 cm to display clear concentration gradients. The application of the pure phase-encoded imaging method effectively addresses this issue and allows imaging of a thinner cell with a shorter Li+ migration path, which allows a faster concentration gradient equilibrium [32]. Results from 7Li MRI and 19F MRI confirm that the distribution of anions and cations within the electrolyte is entirely consistent, adhering to the local electroneutrality rule. Hence, the observation of the higher sensitivity of 19F nuclei’s distribution can enhance signal quality and shorten experimental duration. Combining pure phase-encoded spin-echo chemical shift imaging pulses with diffusion-driven pulses has enabled the acquisition of lithium-ion distribution and diffusion coefficient distribution throughout the electrolyte by a pseudo-3D experiment (Fig. 3d and e). This further verifies the correlation between diffusion coefficients and ion concentration, underscoring the necessity of treating diffusion coefficients and ion migration numbers as functions of concentration when constructing mass transport models to ensure the accuracy of the model [34,35]. Furthermore, under low-temperature conditions, observations indicate electrolyte salt loss during cycling [35], and diffusion limitations occur mainly at the anode side [37]. Additionally, the 19F MRI technique has been demonstrated as a powerful tool for extracting ion transport parameters and constructing mass transport models in high-concentration electrolyte systems [36].

Changes in ion concentration near the electrode region can be directly correlated with the occurrence of interfacial reactions [38,39]. In the case of Si/C anode, 7Li MRI results show an increase in Li+ concentration on the anode surface during the lithiation process, which is attributed to the formation of the solid-electrolyte interface (SEI) [38]. In another case of lithium metal anode, during the lithium metal deposition process within a Li|Li symmetric cell, a significant decrease in anion concentration on the deposited Li surface is observed in 19F MRI, serving as direct evidence of interfacial side reactions and a primary factor in the growth of lithium dendrites. The addition of electrolyte additives effectively mitigates the decomposition of electrolyte salts, thereby suppressing the growth of lithium dendrites [39]. It is worth noting that the studies mentioned above utilized frequency-encoded MRI methods, which have resolution limitations for accurate measurement of electrode surface concentrations.

All-solid-state lithium-ion batteries (ASSLIBs), owing to their bipolar stacking technique and the potential use of lithium metal as the negative electrode, are deemed a highly promising next-generation LIBs with enhanced energy density and better safety [40,41]. The behavior of the solid-state electrolyte, a pivotal component of ASSLIBs, directly influences the energy density, rate capability, and cycle life of the battery via its interfacial evolution and bulk transport properties [42]. MRI technique serves as a powerful tool for investigating both interfacial and bulk properties in ASSLIBs [43–45].

Li10GeP2S12 (LGPS) is currently one of the most advanced solid-state electrolytes., which exhibits high ion conductivity similar to that of liquid electrolytes, but it often leads to cell failure due to the substantial buildup in interfacial impedance during cycling. The main factors are believed to be the formation of SEI at the electrode-electrolyte interface leading to a decline in Li+ concentration. Chien and coworkers employed the 3D 7Li MRI technique to analyze the 2D distribution of lithium ions at the interface and within the bulk of solid-state electrolytes before and after cycling [44]. After three days of electrochemical cycling, 2D cross-sectional images acquired by MRI demonstrated a significant decrease in the uniformity of lithium-ion distribution within the electrolyte, accompanied with the formation of cracks both internally and on the surface of the electrolyte pellet (Fig. 4 ). Calculations of the SNR for various cross-sections revealed that the SNR for the top and bottom cross-sections was only 58% and 82% of that for the internal cross-section, respectively, confirming the correlation between a reduction in lithium-ion concentration at the interface and an increase in overpotential. Following 15 days of electrochemical cycling for LGPS coated with polyethylene oxide (PEO), the SNR for the top and bottom cross-sections increased to 89% and 92% of that for the internal cross-section, respectively. This observation suggests that the PEO coating strategy effectively mitigates the phenomenon of lithium depletion at the interface. It is essential to note that due to the relatively short T
2 for lithium-containing solid samples ( T
2 < 1 ms), frequency encoding in 2D/3D imaging can result in rapid signal decay, resulting in a relatively low SNR during a single scanning process, thereby prolonging data acquisition time. As a result, the total acquisition time for full 3D images in that study reached an unacceptably long duration of 70 h [44].

To enhance the imaging SNR, the observation of more sensitive nuclei (1H, 19F) and their correlation with target nuclei (7Li) is a highly effective strategy. For instance, imaging the lithium-ion distribution in solid-state electrolytes based on organic ionic plastic crystals (OIPCs) can be achieved by focusing on the 1H nucleus [43]. As the Li+ concentration increases within OIPCs, the apparent transverse relaxation time (T 2 ∗) of the 1H nucleus is prolonged, resulting in stronger signals in the image. This allows for the contrast of different Li + concentrations to be obtained in 1H MRI images, and each image acquisition time is only 20–80 min (depending on resolution). MRI results reveal that during the discharge process of a symmetric lithium metal cells with OIPC-based electrolytes, as Li+ from the anode continuously migrate to the OIPC substrate, the metal-electrolyte interface undergoes liquefaction. This liquefaction process enhances ion transport at the interface and the overall performance of the battery.

Researchers also employed MRI technique to study solid-state polymer electrolytes that closely resemble liquid electrolytes [45]. Placing solid-state electrolyte samples with different initial ion concentrations nearby and observing the concentration gradient equilibrium over time, the apparent diffusion coefficient can be determined through a model fit.
The experiment details of MRI characterization of electrolytes for LIBs are summarized in Table 2 .

Lithium metal has long been hailed as the “holy grail” of the anode for LIBs, as it offers both an exceptionally high theoretical specific capacity and the lowest electrode potential, making it a crucial material for achieving high-energy-density LIBs [46]. However, during the deposition process, lithium metal tends to form mossy or dendritic morphology with increased surface area. Such morphology facilitates the electrolyte decomposition and the formation of SEI, and results in the loss of active lithium and short cycle life. On the other hand, dendritic lithium has the potential to puncture the separator, leading to short circuits and thermal runaway, severely compromising the safety performance of the battery [47,48]. Therefore, non-destructive analysis of the morphological evolution of lithium metal is of paramount importance to understanding the formation of Li dendrites.

As previously mentioned, solid samples containing lithium exhibit short T
2 values. Utilizing frequency-encoded imaging methods in such cases results in rapid signal decay and significantly prolongs the experimental duration required to achieve a sufficient SNR. Indirect imaging of a more sensitive nucleus is a classic strategy for improving SNR. Ilott et al. employed 1H 3D FLASH (fast low-angle shot) pulse sequences to image the distribution of protons in a symmetric lithium metal battery. They obtained high-resolution (180 μm × 180 μm × 180 μm) 3D MRI images about the distribution of the electrolyte solvent in a relatively short time (16 min) (Fig. 5 a–c). Through the subtraction of the solvent signal, the distribution of the lithium metal electrode was determined, resulting in rapid and high-resolution lithium metal imaging [49]. Additionally, the pure phase encoding method is an effective way to improve imaging quality. Chemical shift imaging (CSI) is a classic pure-phase encoding imaging method [19]. Its application helps alleviate the rapid decay of the signal caused by magnetic field inhomogeneity, avoids image distortion due to the mismatched magnetic susceptibilities between the bulk and the liquid phase [32], and preserves chemical shift information for the identification of different species. Therefore, CSI is a primary MRI method for lithium metal imaging [50–53].

The interaction between the lithium metal and the RF pulse is worthy of attention. NMR spectroscopy of lithium metal has revealed two interesting phenomena: the skin-depth effect and the bulk magnetization susceptibility (BMS) effect [54,55]. The skin-depth effect refers to the attenuation of RF intensity with an exponential decay as it penetrates a good conductor. The penetration depth δ , so-called “skin depth”, can be calculated using the following formula:
(10)
δ =
ρ
π
μ 0
μ r
ν
where ρ is the electrical resistivity of the conductor, μ
0 and μ
r are the vacuum permeability and relative permeability, respectively, and ν is the characteristic frequency of the RF field. For typical 7Li NMR experiments, the characteristic skin depth of lithium metal is in the range of 10–20 μm. Therefore, for bulk lithium metal, the signal intensity is often proportional to its surface area rather than its volume. Note that the dimensions of mossy lithium and dendritic lithium are normally within the skin depth, which means that the lithium nuclei in such morphologies can be fully excited by the RF, allowing for quantitative analysis [54].

Additionally, when paramagnetic materials are placed in a static magnetic field, they generate an internal magnetic field in the same direction as the static magnetic field. The distribution of magnetic fields in different regions depends on the shape of the paramagnetic material relative to the direction of the static magnetic field [56,57]. For thin lithium metal sheets, when the largest surface area is perpendicular to the static magnetic field, it shows low chemical shift NMR signals around 250 ppm. When this surface is parallel to the static magnetic field, it exhibits a higher chemical shift NMR signal around 270 ppm. Therefore, in practical lithium metal batteries, dendritic lithium and bulk lithium have different chemical shifts due to their orientation differences, allowing them to be distinguished by NMR spectra [54].

Chandrashekar et al. were the first to employ multidimensional MRI techniques to observe the morphology of deposited lithium at both spatial and species scales in a non-invasive manner [50]. 2D MRI imaging results indicated a significant enhancement of the lithium metal signal on the cathode-side surface after the charging process, far stronger than the signal from the bulk. The surface signal enhancement suggests the formation of mossy lithium and dendritic lithium with a larger surface area due to the skin effect. Three-dimension MRI imaging results revealed changes in the thickness of the lithium metal electrode after charging. However, due to limited resolution (240 μm × 160 μm × 2.4 mm) [50], distinguishing fine morphological details of deposited lithium was challenging (Fig. 5d–f). Pseudo-3D CSI imaging compensates for the resolution limitations by introducing a dimension for the chemical shift. The results show that the lithium metal signal on the cathode side exhibited a broadening and shift towards lower chemical shift values compared to the original signal (274 ppm) (Fig. 5e–g). This suggests that some lithium metal grows perpendicular to the direction of the static magnetic field, forming mossy lithium and lithium dendrites. It is worth noting that the signal with a lower chemical shift near the cathode extends toward the anode. This continuous accumulation of lithium microstructure may eventually lead to a short circuit in the battery.

To investigate the growth mechanism of lithium metal in the liquid electrolyte, Chang et al. combined 1D MRI and 1D CSI techniques to study the relationship between the concentration of Li+ in the electrolyte and the morphological evolution of lithium metal [51]. The results indicate that lithium microstructure forms on the cathode surface at the early stages of charging, while the growth of dendritic lithium is influenced by different triggering conditions at varying current densities. When the current density exceeds the critical current density, the depletion of Li+ concentration near the electrode surface triggers the growth of lithium dendrites (Fig. 6 a). This phenomenon confirms the model proposed by Chazalviel [58], indicating that the driving force for dendritic growth under these conditions is slow mass transport within the electrolyte. On the other hand, even when the current density is below the critical current density, dendritic growth can still be observed, suggesting the existence of another mechanism triggering dendrite growth.

Solid-state electrolytes with a certain mechanical strength were once believed to effectively suppress the formation of lithium dendrites [59]. However, short-circuiting often occurs during the cycling of all-solid-state lithium metal symmetric batteries [60]. Non-destructive observation of the morphological evolution of lithium metal during cycling is crucial for elucidating the failure mechanisms of all-solid-state lithium metal batteries. Marbella et al. used 1D CSI technique to observe Li|Li6.5La3Zr1.5Ta0.5O12|Li batteries at different states of charge (Fig. 6b) [52]. The results show that at a current density of 0.5 mA/cm2 during charge and discharge processes, both lithium dissolution on the anode side and lithium deposition on the cathode side are uneven, leading to the formation of lithium microstructures. As cycling progresses, these microstructures gradually accumulate and extend into the interior of the solid-state electrolyte, eventually leading to short-circuiting. It is believed that appropriate stacking pressure may alleviate these issues and extend the cycle life of the battery [61]. Chang et al. investigated the failure mechanisms of Li|Li7La3Zr2O12|Li batteries under different stacking pressures, combining NMR, MRI, and ultrasonic measurement techniques [53]. Their results show that at a stacking pressure of 2 MPa, the dissolution of lithium metal on the anode side continuously forms voids, leading to battery failure. At 7 MPa or higher stacking pressures, interfacial kinetic is dominated by the creep process, which can mitigate the formation of certain lithium microstructure.

Understanding the distribution of Li+ within porous electrodes of LIBs under operating conditions is of great significance and presents a huge challenge. At the particle level, the distribution of Li+ within individual particles is directly related to phase transition during cycling [62,63]. At the electrode level, thick electrodes with high loadings are a straightforward way to increase the energy density of batteries, but the solid-state diffusion process often becomes a rate-determining step for fast-charging performance. Therefore, obtaining information about the distribution of Li+ within electrodes during operation is crucial for studying the solid-state diffusion kinetics of porous electrodes. Compared to lithium metal, it is more challenging to study porous electrodes. Firstly, the lithium content in porous electrodes is significantly lower than in lithium metal, resulting in a weaker signal intensity. Secondly, most porous electrode materials contain paramagnetic ions, further shortening T
2 of the material to less than 100 μs. To obtain high-quality MRI images, researchers often need to use strong magnetic field gradients or specialized imaging techniques.

The stray-field imaging method (STRAFI) relies on the usage of strong magnetic field gradients. By utilizing the rapidly decaying magnetic field at the edge region of a superconducting magnet, researchers can obtain magnetic field gradients several times stronger than those generated by commercial gradient coils. Tang et al. successfully achieved a large magnetic field gradient of 72 T/m within Stray-Field and observed the evolution of the distribution of Li+ in cathode materials such as paramagnetic LiCoO2 and LiFePO4, as well as in graphite anodes during electrochemical processes [64,65]. The results reveal that the delithiation process in the graphite anode is incomplete, with residual Li+ accumulating on the side of the graphite near the separator. A similar phenomenon was not observed in the LiFePO4 cathode.

To mitigate signal attenuation caused by the rapid transverse relaxation of paramagnetic materials, a clever strategy involves substituting the transverse relaxation process with a relatively slower longitudinal relaxation process. Building upon this idea, Tang et al. introduced the scanning image-selected in situ spectroscopy method (S–ISIS) for spatially resolved NMR spectroscopy of paramagnetic electrodes such as Li x CoO2 and Li y Ti5O12 [66]. The S–ISIS approach first involves acquiring the NMR signals across the entire region without magnetic field gradients. Subsequently, when applying a magnetic field gradient, a selective 180° pulse is applied to the target layer by controlling the RF center frequency, causing the magnetization vector of lithium nuclei in the corresponding region to flip to the - z orientation. During this period, the lithium nuclei undergo longitudinal relaxation rather than transverse relaxation, effectively slowing down signal attenuation. After turning off the magnetic field gradient, NMR signals across the entire region are acquired once again. By subtracting the signals before and after, the resulting signal represents the NMR signals of the target region. A significant advantage of this method is the preservation of chemical shift information from various cross-sections. Analyzing the linewidths of different cross-sections allows for an assessment of the lithiation degree of paramagnetic materials (Fig. 7 a–c). The results indicate that during charge and discharge processes, significant concentration gradients are observed within LTO, while lithium ions in LCO remain uniformly distributed, suggesting that the rate-determining step is likely the solid-state diffusion process within LTO.

Krachkovskiy et al. employed a combination of in situ NMR spectroscopy and 1D centric scan single point ramped imaging with T
1 enhancement magnetic resonance imaging method(CSSPRITE-MRI) using pure phase encoding to observe the distribution of Li+ in thick graphite electrodes [67] (Fig. 7d). The advantage of the CSSPRITE-MRI method lies in its ability to avoid image distortions caused by inhomogeneity of the magnetic field and the variations in magnetic susceptibility, while significantly reducing data acquisition time. It's discovered that during the lithiation process, certain lithium ions temporarily reside in the surface layer of the electrode before being inserted into the graphite (Fig. 7e). This results in an increased energy barrier for subsequent Li+ insertion, leading to pronounced concentration gradient. Introducing an additional delithiation step during the lithiation process effectively eliminates the concentration gradient, thereby achieving complete lithiation of the graphite negative electrode. This approach also provides experimental data to support lithium transport models within the insertion material [68].

For porous electrodes, achieving high-resolution MRI imaging often requires extremely high magnetic field gradients and very long experimental time, which are currently the main bottlenecks of this technique (Table 3 ).

The ultimate goal of advanced characterization techniques is to achieve both non-destructive and real-time diagnosis of commercial LIBs. However, traditional NMR and MRI techniques encounter challenges in the direct detection of commercial batteries. Due to the skin effect, RF pulses struggle to penetrate the metallic surface layer of commercial batteries, hindering the acquisition of internal magnetic resonance information. Typically, in situ/real-time NMR or MRI experiments utilize in situ cells without metal film packaging [69], which introduces specific challenges. Firstly, non-metallic barriers in these in situ cells may lead to air infiltration and electrolyte leakage, potentially causing electrochemical processes that are not directly comparable to those in commercial batteries. Secondly, assembling large-capacity in situ cells (>100 mAh) is extremely challenging, thereby limiting the study of internal processes in large-scale batteries.

To directly apply NMR characterization to commercial batteries, Jerschow’s research group has undertaken significant efforts [55,70–78]. To overcome the hindrance posed by the aluminum layer outside the pouch battery, they directly integrated the aluminum layer into the resonant circuit, turning it into a resonator [75]. This innovative approach transformed the aluminum layer from an impediment to RF into an emitter of RF. Using this setup, they successfully acquired NMR signals from the electrolyte, graphite intercalation compounds, and lithium metal within pouch batteries, although constrained by terrible RF field inhomogeneity that results in a lower SNR.

Another interesting concept is that RF does not necessarily need to penetrate the battery’s outer metal layer to detect information from within. MRI technique can be used to analyze the distribution of surface magnetic field outside commercial batteries — a method known as “inside-out MRI” (io-MRI) [70,73] (Fig. 8 a–c). When the battery is operating normally, subtle changes in the surface magnetic field can result from variations in the state of charge (SOC) of the electrode materials [79,80] and the flow of current [81]. These subtle magnetic field changes can be detected by 1H MRI with both high SNR and high spatial resolution. Putting the pouch battery in a detection medium(water), 2D slice images of the magnetic field distribution on both sides of the battery are obtained by observing the signal from the water. As the charging and discharging processes proceed, the changes in the magnetic field around the battery are reversible and symmetrical. Each SOC corresponds to a separate magnetic field distribution map, providing the capability to determine the SOC of an unknown battery [70]. When current flows through the battery in different directions, the magnetic field distribution maps can also be used to diagnose the state of health of the battery [73]. However, the information contained in magnetic field distribution maps is often challenging to decipher due to signal distortions caused by conductive materials and magnetic materials. The introduction of the pure phase encoding SPRITE method effectively eliminates these artifacts [71]. Additionally, replacing liquid detection media with solid detection media can reduce the spatial distance between the medium and the tested battery, making the MRI signal more sensitive to changes in the magnetic field on the battery’s surface [76]. Changes in the surface magnetic field are attributed to three factors: 1) ΔB
SOC resulting from different magnetic susceptibilities of electrode materials at different SOCs, 2) ΔB
DC caused by current flowing through the battery, and 3) ΔB
ST resulting from battery components and inherent configurations. These three components can be separated through experimental measurements with controlled variables. The updated io-MRI technique was employed to analyze overcharged commercial Nokia phone batteries [72]. The ΔB
DC distribution map revealed abnormal magnetic field changes in specific regions of the overcharged battery, potentially related to failure mechanisms caused by overcharging (Fig. 8d–f). Considering the extremely short diagnostic time required (3.5 s), this technique is well-suited for large-scale industrial diagnostic scenarios [77,78]. It is worth noting that the io-MRI method provides indirect information about the internal properties of the battery system. Therefore, specific databases need to be established for different types of batteries, combined with suitable statistical analysis methods, such as principal component analysis [70], histogram analysis [76], and root mean square error analysis [72], to achieve precise diagnosis of target batteries.

MRI has been proven to be a powerful tool for the non-invasive characterization of LIBs. It provides a unique perspective through its combination of spatial resolution and chemical resolution that is challenging to achieve with other characterization techniques. In this article, we focus on the working principles of MRI, thoroughly discuss the characteristics and distinctions among various encoding methods, and identify the suitable research components of LIBs for these methods. The MRI techniques have shown great potential in the study of solid/liquid electrolytes, electrode materials, and commercial batteries. It excels in tasks such as extracting transport properties (e.g., diffusion coefficient D and transference number t ) of electrolytes, monitoring the morphological evolution of lithium metal microstructure, and diagnosing defects in commercial batteries. These capabilities are crucial for optimizing battery performance, investigating internal failure mechanisms, and enabling large-scale, rapid diagnostics of commercial batteries. Furthermore, we have summarized and compared the imaging methods and technical parameters used in various MRI techniques for different researches, aiming to elucidate the current state of technological development and identify potential directions for future optimization.

For liquid electrolytes, 1D frequency encoding methods are the most commonly used experimental techniques to achieve both good signal-to-noise-ratio and a reduced experimental time, facilitating the extraction of the distribution and transport characteristics of Li+. However, the acquisition of 2D/3D distribution information of the liquid electrolyte, especially the electrolyte infiltration within porous electrodes during battery manufacturing [82,83] and the spatial inhomogeneity of electrolyte consumption during battery aging [84], remains a significant research focus and a characterization challenge. 1H/7Li/19F MRI holds the potential for in situ monitoring of the 3D distribution of electrolyte. Given the current limitations in MRI resolution (∼20 μm), which is comparable to the particle size of electrode materials, imaging the distribution of electrolytes within the pores of the electrode remains a challenge. Nevertheless, MRI observing the wetting of the entire battery by the electrolyte at a larger scale is feasible.

For solid samples with short T
2 , such as solid-state electrolytes, lithium metal electrodes, and porous electrodes, phase encoding methods are more commonly used to avoid signal loss due to rapid transverse relaxation in magnetic field gradients. However, both the resolution (>100 μm) and experimental duration (>30 min) for imaging solid samples using the MRI technique are not as good compared to liquid samples. To improve signal quality, current strategies include:
●
More sensitive nuclei: Employing highly sensitive nuclei such as 1H or 19F for imaging reduces acquisition times. The spatial relationship between 7Li and the observed nuclei is utilized for imaging, which effectively reduces the necessary acquisition time for imaging [32,49].
●
Optimized pulse sequences: Advanced pulse sequences such as SPRITE have been widely used in 7Li MRI [35,67]， featuring smaller phase encoding gradients and lower RF duty cycles, which enables efficient signal acquisition [85]. Another effective strategy involves converting rapid transverse relaxation processes into slow longitudinal relaxation processes, a technique known as ISIS [66]. Similar approaches have been applied in 23Na MRI to study the microstructure of sodium metal [86].
●
Additional contrast: Phase encoding often retains frequency information from the FID curve, allowing the chemical shift to serve as an additional dimension in the image [50,51]. Furthermore, the same species in different environments often exhibit different relaxation time constants [87], providing additional contrast in MRI experiments [88].

Considering the current MRI limitations in resolution, imaging along the electrode’s thickness direction may not yield satisfactory results. Nevertheless, MRI is promising to identify the spatial inhomogeneity across the electrode plane, such as variations in the lithium plating regions on graphite surfaces, which plays a crucial role in the uneven aging process of the battery [89,90]. Furthermore, for lithium metal, electron paramagnetic resonance imaging (EPRI) is expected to become a complementary technique to MRI, owing to the larger skin-depth when lithium metal is radiated by microwaves [91,92].

For commercial batteries, current efforts are primarily focused on using NMR spectrometers to perform 1H MRI imaging of the medium surrounding the battery, thereby enabling the subsequent diagnosis of batteries. This approach does not require extremely high spatial resolution, which leads to very short detection times, thereby making it highly suitable for commercial applications. However, this approach is limited by the dimension of the NMR spectrometer cavities and the size of the RF coil. Large medical MRI instruments might potentially serve as powerful tools for diagnosing battery faults. Additionally, an alternative approach involves direct measurement of the surface magnetic fields of batteries using magnetometers. However, since magnetometers cannot provide magnetic field measurements with spatial resolution, they often require a positioning system to move the sample [93,94]. A very recent report highlighted that for relatively simple single-layer stack batteries, the use of an array of magnetic sensors can accurately diagnose the battery’s current distribution and defect positions within 1.1 min [95], demonstrating significant commercial potential for such techniques.

In conclusion, while MRI technology has undergone significant development and is widely used in clinical medicine, its application in the field of LIBs remains an emerging research area. The capability of MRI to access deeper information within LIBs hinges on advancements in hardware and improvements in imaging methods. We anticipate that MRI techniques with enhanced resolution and sensitivity will soon play a pivotal role in unraveling the hidden complexities within batteries.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hongxin Lin received his Bachelor’s degree in Sun Yat-sen University in 2021. He is now pursuing his Ph.D. in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University supervised by Prof. Yong Yang. His research focuses on the study and application of solid-state NMR and MRI methods in the energy storage systems like lithium-ion batteries.

Yong Yang is a distinguished professor in Chemistry in the State Key Lab for Physical Chemistry of Solid Surface at Xiamen University since 1997. He now also serves as Editor for J Power Sources, The President of International Battery Materials Association (IBA) (2024–2025) and Board Member of International Meeting of Lithium Battery (IMLB). His main recent research interests are Solid-State batteries, Li-ion Battery Ageing and Degradation Analysis for Power and Storage Applications and operando techniques such as NMR/MRI. He has published 500+ referred papers with H = 88 (Google Scholar). He also obtained several awards such as Technology Award in Battery Division, ECS in 2020.
